# Questions in Livestream 205

Live Stream #205: Dead Seminaries Society

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=463" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:43</a> | What should someone do if they were accidentally baptized in an invalid way but their church will not rebaptize them and another church is not available? | He said the church should provide some path forward if it is really declaring the earlier baptism invalid, and he also stressed that baptism is not what saves. | He distinguished invalid baptism from minor denominational objections, noting that many churches historically accepted baptisms even from heretical Christian groups. If a church truly says the baptism was invalid but refuses any valid remedy, he thought the church is being inconsistent and the person may have to seek another church if possible, while remembering that salvation does not depend on baptism itself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=882" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:42</a> | How can the sun be over Gibeon in Joshua 10:12 if Gibeon is north of the tropics? | He read the passage as an omen-laden astronomical sign for the Amorites, not as a simple modern-scientific description. | He said the text is not claiming the sun was directly overhead or literally stationary in the modern sense. He interpreted the scene as an astrological posture, with sun and moon visible in opposition, using language comparable to ancient omen texts rather than a statement about tropical latitude. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=1046" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:26</a> | Why was the sun-and-moon sign special enough to write down? | He said it was special because it would have been a bad omen for the Amorites, attacking their morale through their own astrological assumptions. | The sign mattered because the Amorites would have read it as a disastrous omen, so Joshua's attack took place on a day they would regard as deeply unfavorable. He compared it to attacking someone on a day they superstitiously fear, emphasizing that the narrative uses their astrological worldview against them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=1195" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:55</a> | Are zombies real? | Reanimated corpses are not real, but he said Haitian-style zombification using toxins to create mindless living servants is a real phenomenon. | He separated fictional undead bodies from living people drugged into a deathlike or compliant state. In that latter sense, he said zombification has been discussed as applied pharmacology involving toxins, but it is not resurrection or a corpse returning to life. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=1315" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:55</a> | How should Exodus 20:4-6 be understood if it seems to prohibit all images while God commands cherubim on the ark? | He said the commandment prohibits worshiped idols, not all artwork or decorative sacred images. | He argued that the Hebrew terms in Exodus 20 refer to idols or cult images made for worship and service, not every visual representation. The cherubim on the ark and other sacred art are therefore lawful because they mark holiness and sacred space without being treated as gods. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=1636" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:16</a> | Were almond flowers and pomegranates used in temple iconography? | Yes. He said they, like cherubim, were lawful temple images because they were not objects of worship. | He confirmed that almond flowers, pomegranates, and cherubim belonged to temple iconography. Their function was symbolic and decorative, representing purity or holiness, and the decisive issue was whether anyone worshiped or served the image. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=1698" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:18</a> | What is your opinion on images or icons of Christ? | He saw most icon use as appeal or invocation rather than worship, though he did not think icons were efficacious and warned against worshiping them. | He treated icons of Christ as a more complicated case because Christ is God, but he said most actual practice is not worship of the icon itself. He described it more as appeal or invocation through the person represented, did not think icons are especially effective, and warned that worship or service directed to an image would cross into idolatry. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=1945" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:25</a> | How should someone read Titus Kennedy's book *Excavating the Evidence for Jesus*, if at all? | He said Kennedy's books are shallow but have useful photos, and that the Jesus book is better than the Old Testament one. | He advised enjoying the photographs but not expecting depth from the archaeology. He thought the Jesus volume was substantially better than Kennedy's Old Testament book, partly because New Testament archaeology is easier to present, but still characterized the books as not very useful beyond their images. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=2109" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:09</a> | How can you write historical fiction about periods when the church did horrible things without whitewashing the atrocities or pushing readers away from Christianity? | He said the key is to tell the truth about the wrongdoing while refusing to equate Christ with every misuse of Christ's name. | He urged honest, nuanced writing: do not soften atrocities, but also show those who resisted or acted faithfully in the same period. He said strong historical fiction gives both villains and resisters real motives, showing how people justify evil without making Christ responsible for every act done under Christian language. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=2580" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:00</a> | Would you be interested in doing a future video on Gnostic gospels? | Not especially. He said he finds them dull and not very rich in actual gnostic thought. | He said Gnostic gospels often are rewritten or reinterpreted forms of other gospel material rather than clear presentations of secret doctrine. Because Gnostics prized hidden knowledge, he argued that much of what they believed was not written down, making the texts less informative than people expect and difficult to turn into an interesting video. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=3319" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:19</a> | How do you study the Bible, such as the epistles; do you use an exegetical or hermeneutical checklist? | He said he does not use a checklist but practices attentive reading, looking carefully at context, language, and connections while using commentaries when helpful. | He described pausing over each sentence and looking for context, subtext, textual notes, ancient Near Eastern connections, language details, and links to other biblical passages. He warned against merely getting through the text or reducing reading to a checklist, and recommended commentaries as a normal aid when context is unclear. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=3507" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:27</a> | Are dinosaurs real? | Yes. He said dinosaurs were real, though they are now extinct. | His answer was brief and direct: dinosaurs existed, but they are extinct. He treated this as a simple factual question rather than a point needing extended qualification. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=3525" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:45</a> | Who are the Emim and Zamzummim in Deuteronomy 2? | He treated them as old regional giant-clan or people-group labels preserved in the text's historical memory. | He connected the Emim and Zamzummim with older regional peoples remembered in Deuteronomy alongside groups like the Anakim and Rephaim. His answer treated the names as preserved ethnic or clan labels associated later with the giant traditions, not as groups the audience should overdefine from the limited data. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=3738" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:18</a> | Does Deuteronomy 2:10 imply the Anakim were huge? | Not strongly. He said it is a scribal gloss and later language made groups like Rephaim and Anakim synonymous with giants. | He cautioned that the verse looks like an explanatory gloss and should not carry more weight than it can bear. In his reading, later scribal and traditional language tends to merge names such as Rephaim and Anakim with giant traditions, but that does not by itself prove a precise physical size. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=3784" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:03:04</a> | Are trees the only things hard to date in the Levant? | He said wood is especially hard because of regional carbon and tree-ring issues, while annual crops and fabrics are easier when preserved. | He said wood is the hardest common material to date in the Mediterranean basin because old carbon and tree-ring issues complicate radiocarbon results. By contrast, short-lived annual crops, textiles, and similar materials are often better if they survive, because they are less affected by long growth or reuse problems. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=3960" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:00</a> | How are Matthew 5:38-39 related? | He said Jesus contrasts lex talionis with nonretaliation, moving from legal limits on revenge toward loving neighbor and enemy. | He explained verse 38 as the law of retribution, a maximum legal limit rather than permission to escalate revenge. Jesus then contrasts that legal limit with the call to absorb insult and avoid retaliation, pressing the listener toward love of neighbor and enemy rather than answering every wrong in kind. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=4291" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:31</a> | Is Manasseh called a half-tribe because half lived across the Jordan or because of Joseph and Ephraim? | He said it is because Joseph's inheritance was divided into Ephraim and Manasseh, not because of the Jordan settlement pattern. | He said Joseph's inheritance is doubled and divided between Ephraim and Manasseh, leaving no separate tribe of Joseph in the same sense. The half-tribe language therefore refers to that division of Joseph's inheritance, not to Manasseh being split by the Jordan. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=4369" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:49</a> | When are you going back to Egypt? | He was unsure whether another tour would happen because the previous tour had low signups and last-minute cancellations, but said it might be a year or two if it happens. | He said the previous tour had only a small number of actual signups after much more interest was expressed, and cancellations caused the tour company to lose money. Because of that, he could not promise another trip, though he floated a possible one- or two-year horizon if a future tour became viable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=4457" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:17</a> | Were the tribes on the east side of the Jordan only to return when all the other tribes conquered the land, even though some never did? | He said he would need the exact text, but tentatively took it as the land the tribes were actually going to conquer, not every promised tract in an absolute sense. | Without the exact passage in front of him, he answered cautiously. He suggested the statement likely means the western tribes finished conquering the land they were actually going to possess, not that every theoretically promised tract had to be subdued before the eastern tribes could return. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=4620" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:00</a> | How do you move away from fundamentalism without abandoning the faith? | He answered by separating the core of the faith from the habits and assumptions that define fundamentalism. | He defined fundamentalism mainly as a wooden, traditional, literalistic hermeneutic and said many atheist debunking channels keep that same reading even after leaving the faith. His advice was to learn more nuanced readings, use reliable commentaries, consider ancient context and grammar, and discover that the core message of Scripture remains coherent even when simplistic readings are abandoned. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=5189" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:29</a> | How important is biblical inerrancy to the Bible being considered God's word, and is that a false dichotomy? | He said inerrancy is important and foundational, especially for sola scriptura, but not strictly necessary for calling the Bible God's word. | He affirmed the Chicago-style idea that God's word is without error in the inspired autographs, while allowing that one can call the Bible God's word without having fully worked out inerrancy. Still, he thought inerrancy becomes especially important wherever a church relies on Scripture as final authority, because that retreat position needs a strong account of Scripture's trustworthiness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=5695" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:55</a> | Which archaeology book would David gift, apart from his own? | He recommended James K. Hoffmeier's *The Archaeology of the Bible* as a beautiful and useful gift book. | He recommended Hoffmeier's book because it is visually strong, accessible, and also useful as a summary of archaeology and the Bible. He described it as a good gift or coffee-table book with enough substance to be worthwhile beyond the photographs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=5909" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:29</a> | Were the sacrifices in Leviticus 1:6 similar to sacrifices in Egypt during that time? | He said not really, though both systems could feed priests; Egyptian offerings were usually presented and redistributed rather than handled like Levitical burnt offerings. | He said Egyptian sacrifices were typically made into food, presented before the gods, and then eaten or redistributed by priests, rather than burned in the Levitical way. The main similarity was that both systems could support the priesthood, but the handling of meat and grain offerings differed in important ritual details. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=6094" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:34</a> | Is it true that children made to pass through the fire were then eaten? | No. He said this referred to child sacrifice as a burnt offering, with no evidence of cannibalism. | He identified passing through the fire as a euphemism for child sacrifice by burning. He described remains being collected in urns and buried in a tophet, using Carthage as an example, and said there is no evidence of cannibalism in that practice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=6179" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:59</a> | Is passing children through the fire Moloch? | He said Moloch appears to refer to a kind of child-sacrifice offering, not to a known ancient Near Eastern god. | He said current evidence does not establish a known deity named Moloch in the ancient Near East. On that basis, he treated the term as referring to the sacrifice itself, specifically a burnt child-sacrifice offering, rather than the name of the god receiving it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=6222" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:42</a> | What is your favorite biblical archaeological find? | He chose the Merneptah Victory Stela because of its early reference to Israel, firm date, and useful political context. | He favored the Merneptah Victory Stela because it gives an early datable reference to Israel in Canaan, around Merneptah's fifth regnal year. He also valued how it works with other Egyptian evidence to illuminate Merneptah's political pressures, Libyan conflict, and the setting in which Israel could enter the land. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=6357" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:57</a> | If Leviticus foreshadows the sacrifice of Jesus, how can we avoid missing its message to its original audience? | He recommended a Christotelic approach: start with the original audience and context, then move to Christ as the fulfillment. | He said readers should first ask what Leviticus meant in its own context and to its original audience. Only after that should they move toward Christ as the telos or fulfillment, which he thought preserves the original meaning better than beginning with a Christocentric reading that skips the earlier context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=6565" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:25</a> | What year is most likely for Christ's crucifixion and resurrection? | He said AD 30 is probably more likely than AD 33, though those are the main possible years in the relevant Passover and Sabbath calculations. | He discussed the Thursday meal, Friday crucifixion, sunset-to-sunset Jewish reckoning, and the years in which Passover and Sabbath timing can align. He said AD 30 and AD 33 are the major candidates, with AD 30 probably favored by most scholars in his view. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=6955" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:55</a> | What is going on in Mark 14:51-52 with the young man who fled naked? | He said nobody knows for sure; it could be Mark inserting himself or a literary representation of the disciples fleeing. | He said the identity of the young man is unknown and noted that the question comes up often. He mentioned possible explanations, including Mark referring to himself or the scene serving as a literary image of the disciples' abandonment, but did not treat either as certain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=7083" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:03</a> | What did the Jews do about dirty diapers? | He said they cleaned them, as everyone did before disposable diapers, and it was not a major ritual issue. | He treated it as an ordinary domestic problem rather than a special ritual category. People cleaned cloths and waste as all pre-disposable societies had to do, and he did not see the question as creating a major purity-law difficulty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=7156" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:16</a> | Why does Mary being full of grace in Luke 1:28 mean she was sinless, when Stephen is also called full of grace in some translations? | He said it does not mean sinless; it means Mary was highly favored, and reading sinlessness from the phrase is eisegesis. | He argued that the phrase indicates divine favor, not inherent sinlessness. He warned against turning sufficiency into necessity, saying later theological readings may find Mary fitting for a role without the wording itself requiring the doctrine being read into it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=7845" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:45</a> | If manna is mushrooms, does that contradict John 6:31-35? | No. He said "from heaven" means God-delivered, and Exodus describes manna appearing after dew evaporates rather than visibly falling from the sky. | He said "from heaven" in John and Exodus means divine provision, not necessarily that the substance visibly fell from the sky. Exodus 16 describes dew evaporating and a fine flake-like thing appearing on the ground, which he thought can fit a natural delivery mechanism under God's providence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=8616" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:23:36</a> | What is the difference between our soul and our spirit, or are they the same thing? | He said both bipartite and tripartite views have arguments, and he does not hold a firm settled answer. | He presented two major options: a simpler body-and-soul view and a tripartite body, soul, and spirit view. He found both attractive for different reasons, including the possibility that soul and spirit could be distinct aspects in dialogue, but said he does not settle firmly on one. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=8771" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:11</a> | Did priests see a theophany, light, or fire between the cherubim, and how could Nadab and Abihu trespass if God was visibly there? | He said there was normally no visible fire or light between the cherubim, and Nadab and Abihu likely acted carelessly with strange fire rather than seeing a constant visible warning. | He said God's presence was understood to be between the cherubim, but normally not visibly manifested as fire or light except in special moments such as the tabernacle's dedication. He framed Nadab and Abihu's trespass as careless ritual corner-cutting, probably involving unclean fire and improper handling, not defiance in front of a constant visible theophany. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=9013" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:13</a> | Could Yahweh be derived from adding an H to the end because Hebrew did not like final vowels except for possessive pronouns? | He said that is possible, and that Yahweh, Yahoo, and Yah could relate through several shortening or spelling patterns. | He said that kind of derivation is plausible because Hebrew does tend to avoid final vowels. He allowed several related possibilities, including Yahweh being shortened to Yahoo or Yah, or an H being added in a spelling or phonological development. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=9107" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:31:47</a> | Were Midianites worshipers of Yahoo while later Hebrew made it the full tetragrammaton? | He said we do not know because we lack Midianite writings; the main evidence is the biblical material about Moses and his father-in-law. | He said the idea would be exciting if direct Midianite evidence existed, but it does not. The only real evidence he was willing to use is the biblical tradition involving Moses in Midian and his father-in-law, so he refused to claim more than the evidence supports. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=9238" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:33:58</a> | What is Qos? | He described Qos as the great god of Edom, attested in limited seals or inscriptions, with uncertain etymology and an unclear relationship to Edomite monotheistic tendencies. | He said Qos appears in some Edomite names, seals, and limited inscriptions, but the evidence remains thin and the name's etymology is uncertain. He also noted that Edom's religion seems to have been treated differently from some neighboring polytheisms in biblical material, which makes Qos an intriguing but underdocumented figure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=9421" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:01</a> | How can Christians pursue justice in a corrupt country if personal vengeance is not allowed? | He said Christians should seek legal redress where possible, act through love and prayer, and leave vengeance or final justice to the Lord when earthly justice fails. | He said Christians should pursue justice through law, prayer, and love of neighbor rather than personal vengeance. If the legal order is too corrupt to deliver justice, believers may have to wait for the Lord's justice, trusting God while still praying for enemies and refusing revenge. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=9589" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:49</a> | Why was Samson allowed and blessed to get personal vengeance? | He said Samson was a judge and a tool, not a moral model, and that the blessing was tied to the Nazarite vow rather than an endorsement of all his actions. | He said Samson's role as judge made him an instrument God used, but that does not make Samson exemplary or prescribe his behavior for believers. The blessing was connected to the Nazarite vow, and the narrative often describes permitted actions without endorsing them morally. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=9878" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:38</a> | What do you make of the claim that Yam Suph is a judgment against Yam from the Baal Cycle? | He rejected the argument, saying it forces the Baal Cycle onto an Exodus itinerary made up of real, documented places. | He said he had heard a similar argument and rejected it because it treats the itinerary as mythological when the places are documented in Egyptian sources. In his view, the theory depends on cosmetic name similarities and breaks down once the route's actual geography is considered. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=10169" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:29</a> | Can we make a geopolitical case for the Exodus in light of Egypt, the Hittites, and the Battle of Kadesh? | Yes. He connected a late Exodus and Israel's entry to Egypt's civil conflict, Hittite collapse, Philistine pressure, and late Bronze destruction patterns. | He placed Israel's entry into the end of the Late Bronze Age, when Egypt was weakening, Merneptah's period led into civil conflict, the Hittite empire was collapsing, and the Philistines had already taken the coastal plain. He said those pressures made room for movement across the Arnon and Heshbon and fit late Bronze destruction and early Israelite settlement patterns. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=10381" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:53:01</a> | Will *Reliquary of the Dead* be a 2D adventure game or an FMV game? | He said there are no plans to make it a video game; an audiobook or television adaptation would be more plausible if the book sold well enough. | He said there are no plans to turn the book into any kind of game and joked about not liking that style of gameplay. If anything, he thought an audiobook would be the next realistic format if sales allowed it, with a television adaptation only as a much larger rights and production possibility. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=11001" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:21</a> | Who is the most likable character in the book? | He and the producer pointed to Mahat or Melissa, with joking mentions of the cobra and scatterbug. | The answer was informal and tied to the novel discussion rather than a doctrinal topic. He and the producer leaned toward Mahat or Melissa as the most likable, while also joking with the audience about other memorable characters and props from the story. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=11156" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:05:56</a> | What is the likely date range for the Gospel of John? | He said most scholars place it around 80-95 AD, while deferring to D. A. Carson for details. | He gave the common scholarly range of roughly AD 80 to 95 and did not claim specialist expertise on Johannine dating. For a more detailed argument, he deferred to D. A. Carson as a stronger authority on the Gospel of John. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=11222" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:07:02</a> | Is the "two powers in heaven" idea evident elsewhere in the Old Testament beyond Daniel 7? | Yes. He pointed to Genesis 1, Babel, the angel of the Lord, and Isaiah 44:6. | He said the pattern appears in multiple Old Testament texts, including God's Spirit in Genesis 1, the plural language around creation and Babel, and angel-of-the-Lord passages. He also cited Isaiah 44:6, where he read the Lord, king of Israel, and his redeemer, the Lord of hosts, as a clear two-powers-style formulation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=11345" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:05</a> | If Josephus and Manetho got the Exodus pharaoh wrong, what evidence do early-date advocates have for Amenhotep II? | He said Manetho traditions conflict, and without them the case mostly rests on a contested reading of 1 Kings 6:1. | He said the Manetho tradition survives in conflicting forms naming different pharaohs, so using one version is cherry-picking. Without that support, he thought early-date advocates mainly lean on their reading of 1 Kings 6:1, and even that does not straightforwardly get them to Amenhotep II. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=11666" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:14:26</a> | Who was the Semitic chariot driver for Ramesses II? | He said we do not know; Kadesh material mentions a driver but not by name, and such a person would likely have been highly trusted. | He said Ramesses II could drive a chariot, but if someone else drove for him, that person is not named in the Kadesh material. He assumed such a driver would have been highly trusted and likely high-ranking, but he would not identify the person without evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=11846" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:17:26</a> | What makes a doctrine or belief actually heretical? | He said heresy must compromise an essential Christian doctrine, usually about God, humanity, salvation, or core dogma; secondary disagreements can be wrong without being heretical. | He defined heresy as a belief that compromises salvation by attacking core doctrines such as the nature of God, the nature of humanity, the Trinity, salvation by grace through faith, the virgin conception, the fall, the atonement, or similar essentials. Academic or secondary disputes can be wrong, even seriously wrong, without being heretical. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=12401" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:26:41</a> | What if you do not have the evidence to hand? | He said following the evidence means not speaking beyond it; possible future evidence should be framed as hope or uncertainty, not treated as proof. | He insisted that evidence-based argument stops where the evidence stops. It is acceptable to hope future discoveries may clarify something, but irresponsible to stretch current evidence, present conjecture as fact, or claim vindication before the evidence exists. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=12924" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:35:24</a> | What was the name of God known as at the Tower of Babel: Anu, El, or Jehovah? | He said we do not know, but *ilum* is a plausible generic guess if the setting involved Akkadian or a West Semitic dialect. | He said the text does not tell us what divine name people used at Babel. If pressed for a historically plausible generic term, he suggested something like *ilum* in an Akkadian or West Semitic setting, but he framed that as a guess rather than recoverable certainty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=13061" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:37:41</a> | Is whispering in tombs common practice among Egyptologists and archaeologists, or only among people with tourist-group degrees? | He said whispering is not normally required unless other people are in a public tomb and you are trying not to disturb them. | He said Egyptologists and archaeologists do not normally whisper in tombs as a professional rule. The only ordinary reason to lower one's voice would be courtesy in a public tomb when other people are nearby. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=13155" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:39:15</a> | Is there proof or evidence that the port of Avaris was used to leave Egypt rather than going with Moses, and where would the people go? | He said no; the port route would head through a controlled choke point toward the Mediterranean, while the Exodus route fits movement south through Pi-Ramesses and Wadi Tumilat. | He said there is no evidence for an exodus by the port of Avaris, and practically it would not move an entire population well. A water route would have to pass a guarded choke point at Charu and lead toward the Mediterranean and Philistia, while the biblical route better fits a movement south from Avaris or Pi-Ramesses through Wadi Tumilat and then toward the wilderness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=13417" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:43:37</a> | Are 1 Samuel 16:14-23 and chapter 17 out of chronological order, or is there another way to read them? | He did not see a clear chronological disorder, though he allowed that details such as David's armor-bearer status could be retrojected. | After reading the passage, he said nothing immediately required seeing chapters 16 and 17 as chronologically reversed. The only possible tension he noted was David's armor-bearer status, but he thought that could reflect servant protocol, limited familiarity, or later retrojection without forcing a disorder. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=13765" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:49:25</a> | Could you discuss obeying God versus doing good works? | He said good works do not save, but God prepares good works for believers to walk in, and those works perfect faith. | He tied Ephesians 2 and James 2 together: salvation is by grace through faith, not works, yet believers are created in Christ for good works God prepared beforehand. Works are therefore the fruit and perfection of faith, not the basis on which someone is saved. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=14116" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:55:16</a> | Do we have a guardian angel or guardian angels? | He said Matthew 18 gives warrant for guardian angels, especially for children, but Scripture does not say whether guardians are personal or shared. | He cited Matthew 18's language about the angels of the little ones as warrant for believing angels protect God's beloved, especially children. He said Scripture does not specify whether each person has a personal guardian or whether angels are assigned more broadly to groups. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9X7hVNRfNUw?t=14385" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:59:45</a> | What is the history and tradition behind gargoyles on Roman Catholic and Anglican churches? | He said gargoyles are medieval mockery of devils or demons, meant to make prideful evil ridiculous. | He explained gargoyles as a form of medieval humor directed against evil: since Satan's great sin is pride, the images mock and deform demons to make them ridiculous. The point is not reverence for the figures but public humiliation of evil through grotesque display. |
